title: Why Men Need to Step Up Emotionally at Home (Not Just Financially)
guestBio: Bart Morse is the founder of the Lion's Den Program, a transformational coaching program for men. He specializes in helping men move beyond traditional masculine roles of providing and protecting to become true emotional leaders in their relationships and families.
excerpt: The first male guest on The Manage Her reveals why the mental load crisis in marriage isn't just a women's issue—and shares his groundbreaking framework for men to step into true partnership at home.
metaDescription: Discover why men need to move beyond providing to emotional leadership at home. Expert insights on sharing mental load and creating true partnership in marriage.
keyTakeaways: True masculine leadership at home goes far beyond providing financially—it requires emotional presence and shared responsibility for family systems.
The 'nice guy' syndrome and people-pleasing behaviors actually create distance in relationships rather than the safety they're intended to provide.
Boundaries are external limits while principles are internal values—understanding this distinction transforms how couples create safety together.
Men can begin sharing the mental load by taking ownership of family calendars, systems, and forward-thinking planning.
The '8 Mountains' framework provides a holistic approach to fulfillment that encompasses marriage, parenting, health, finances, and personal growth.
Power struggles in marriage transform into partnership when both people commit to radical ownership of their roles and responsibilities.
Shame is often the root barrier preventing men from stepping into deeper emotional leadership and authentic vulnerability.

pullQuotes.text: True leadership at home isn't just about providing and protecting—it's about emotionally showing up for your family every single day.

timestamps.label: The Mental Load Crisis in Marriage
Why Men Default to Provider Mode

content:
What if the mental load crisis plaguing marriages isn't just a women's issue? What if the solution requires men to fundamentally redefine what leadership looks like at home? In this groundbreaking episode, Aimee sits down with Bart Morse, founder of the Lion's Den Program and the very first male guest on The Manage Her, to explore these exact questions.

The conversation tackles one of the most pressing issues facing modern couples: the invisible weight that women carry in managing households, relationships, and family systems. But rather than simply acknowledging the problem, Bart offers a radical reframe of masculine responsibility that goes far beyond the traditional roles of provider and protector.

Moving Beyond Provider Mode

Many men have been conditioned to believe that their primary value in a relationship comes from their ability to provide financially and protect their families from external threats. While these roles have historical importance, Bart argues that they fall dramatically short of what true partnership requires in modern marriage. Real leadership at home demands emotional presence, forward-thinking planning, and shared responsibility for the countless decisions and systems that keep a family running smoothly.

This shift requires men to develop what Bart calls emotional leadership skills. Instead of waiting for their partners to manage schedules, anticipate needs, or navigate family dynamics, emotionally mature men proactively engage with these responsibilities. They don't just ask "how can I help?"—they take ownership of seeing what needs to be done and doing it.

The Eight Mountains Framework

Central to Bart's approach is his Eight Mountains framework, a holistic model for masculine fulfillment that encompasses marriage, parenting, physical health, mental health, finances, career, friendships, and relationship with God or higher purpose. This comprehensive approach recognizes that men who are struggling in their marriages are often struggling in multiple areas of life simultaneously.

The framework challenges the compartmentalized thinking that allows men to excel professionally while neglecting their emotional development or family responsibilities. True fulfillment requires intentional growth across all eight domains, with each mountain supporting and strengthening the others.

Confronting the Nice Guy Syndrome

One of the most eye-opening aspects of the conversation centers on what Bart identifies as the "nice guy syndrome." Contrary to popular belief, people-pleasing behaviors and conflict avoidance don't create safety in relationships—they create distance and frustration. When men consistently defer to their partners' preferences or avoid difficult conversations in an attempt to "keep the peace," they inadvertently burden their spouses with even more decision-making responsibility.

This pattern often stems from shame and fear of disappointing others. Men caught in this cycle may believe they're being considerate, but they're actually abdicating their leadership responsibilities and forcing their partners to carry even more of the mental load. Breaking free requires developing the courage to have difficult conversations, express genuine preferences, and take stands on issues that matter.

Boundaries Versus Principles

Bart introduces a powerful distinction between boundaries and principles that reframes how couples can create safety together. Boundaries are external limits we set with other people about what behavior we will and won't accept. Principles, on the other hand, are internal values that guide our own behavior regardless of what others do.

While boundaries are important, an over-reliance on them can create a defensive, adversarial dynamic in marriage. Principles offer a different path forward—when both partners commit to living by strong internal principles of respect, integrity, and love, they create safety through their own consistent behavior rather than through external controls.

Practical Partnership Strategies

The conversation doesn't stop at philosophy—it offers concrete strategies for men who want to step into deeper partnership. Bart suggests starting with family calendar management, taking ownership of forward-thinking planning, and developing systems that prevent last-minute scrambling. This might mean being the person who notices when kids need new clothes, schedules doctor appointments, or plans ahead for upcoming school events.

He also emphasizes the importance of vision alignment. Many couples operate from day to day without ever discussing their long-term goals and dreams. Men can lead by initiating these conversations and working collaboratively with their partners to create shared vision for their family's future.

The Ripple Effect

Perhaps most powerfully, the episode explores how this work extends far beyond individual marriages. When men step into true emotional leadership at home, they model healthy masculinity for their sons and daughters. They demonstrate what partnership looks like and break generational patterns of emotional unavailability or over-functioning women.

This transformation doesn't happen overnight, and it requires men to confront uncomfortable truths about their current patterns and behaviors. But for those willing to do the work, the rewards extend to every area of life—stronger marriages, more connected relationships with children, and a deeper sense of purpose and fulfillment.

Whether you're a woman feeling overwhelmed by the mental load in your relationship or a man ready to step into deeper responsibility and partnership, this conversation offers both inspiration and practical tools for creating the kind of marriage and family life you truly want. The time for half-measures and surface-level changes has passed—true transformation requires nothing less than a complete redefinition of what masculine leadership looks like in the modern home.

quiz.title: What's Your Partnership Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally approach shared responsibility and emotional leadership in your closest relationships. This quiz will reveal insights about your unique strengths and growth opportunities.

questions.question: When you notice something important needs to happen in your household or relationship, what's your typical first move?

options.text: I start thinking through all the steps needed and begin organizing a plan to make it happen
I ask what I can do to help and look for ways to support the effort
I bring it up directly and want to talk through what's really going on underneath
I start doing small things to model the change I want to see

questions.question: How do you typically handle it when you're feeling overwhelmed by everything on your plate?

options.text: I make lists and try to create better systems so this doesn't happen again
I push through and focus on what others need from me first
I need to talk it out with someone who will really listen and understand
I try to simplify and let go of things that aren't absolutely essential

questions.question: When there's tension or unspoken frustration in your relationship, you're most likely to:

options.text: Try to figure out what systems or routines might solve the underlying issue
Look for concrete ways to help reduce stress and show you care
Address it head-on because avoiding it will only make things worse
Give it some space while being extra thoughtful about your own actions

questions.question: What energizes you most about being in a committed partnership?

options.text: Building something meaningful together and seeing how you complement each other's strengths
Being someone your partner can always count on and knowing they feel supported
Having deep conversations and really knowing each other at the core level
Creating a peaceful, harmonious life where you both feel free to be yourselves

questions.question: When your partner expresses frustration about household or family management, your instinct is to:

options.text: Ask specific questions about what systems or processes could work better
Immediately offer to take things off their plate and ask how you can help
Want to understand what they're really feeling and what this means for your relationship
Listen carefully and then quietly start taking on more without making a big deal about it

questions.question: How do you prefer to create positive change in your relationships?

options.text: Through clear communication about roles and creating structures that work for everyone
By consistently showing up and proving your commitment through actions
By having honest conversations about what's working and what isn't
Through small, steady changes that naturally build momentum over time

results.title: The Systems Builder
results.description: You naturally think ahead and love creating structure that makes life flow smoothly. You're the person others count on to remember the details and keep things organized. Your superpower is seeing the bigger picture and building systems that support everyone. This episode will show you how to leverage your natural planning abilities to create even deeper partnership while helping others step up alongside you.

results.title: The Steady Supporter
results.description: You excel at being present and responsive when others need you. Your strength lies in your reliability and willingness to jump in wherever help is needed. You create stability through your consistent care and attention to what matters most to your people. This episode will help you understand how to maintain your supportive nature while also establishing boundaries that create space for others to take more ownership.

results.title: The Authentic Connector
results.description: You value genuine communication and aren't afraid to address the real issues in relationships. Your strength is creating emotional safety through honesty and vulnerability. You naturally see through surface-level solutions to the deeper dynamics at play. This episode will resonate with your desire for authentic partnership and give you language for the changes you know need to happen.

results.title: The Gentle Influencer
results.description: You prefer to lead through example and create change gradually rather than through confrontation. Your strength is maintaining harmony while still moving relationships forward. You understand that lasting change happens through patience and consistent modeling. This episode will give you practical strategies for creating the shifts you want to see while honoring your natural approach to influence.

transcript:
**Aimee:** Hi everyone and welcome back to The Manage Her, the podcast where we reframe the invisible work of women as powerful leadership. I'm your host Aimee Rickabus, author, entrepreneur, mama of six and advocate for every woman who's ever carried the mental load. Today we're flipping the script a bit because for the first time on this show, we're welcoming a male guest. But trust me, he's not just any guest.

Bart Morse is the founder of the Lion's Den program, a transformational coaching experience designed to help men become better partners, fathers, and leaders. He's worked with tech giants like Microsoft and T-Mobile. But his real genius lies in helping men drop their defenses, take ownership, and step into authentic emotional leadership. If you've ever wished the men in your life had a manual for how to show up with more clarity, presence, and accountability, this episode is for you. We're talking about what it takes to build trust, shift household dynamics, and build real partnership at home. Let's get into it.

Hey, Bart. Nice to have you on the show. Thank you so much for showing up for me today.

**Bart:** Thank you, Aimee. Great to be here. It's a real honor.

**Aimee:** Yeah. Well, you're our very first man on the show. So this is new territory here for the podcast.

**Bart:** That is an honor.

**Aimee:** Yes, I know. The very first man. So, as you know, or maybe you don't, but most of our listeners are women and many of them are carrying a lot of emotional and mental labor at home. How do you help men begin to recognize the weight that their partners are carrying and how to take real responsibility for that?

**Bart:** That's a great question. A lot of the men that come to me and what I do - the program that I run was born out of helping men in marital crisis. So oftentimes they've heard the word separation or divorce, but not always. Sometimes they just know that they need to level up. So that's an important context, because if you're talking to a man who's not going through that, he's probably going to have a different attention span for what I might say.

When a man realizes that his marriage is on the line, he's a lot more open to kind of receiving what I have to say. Essentially, what I would say is a lot of men realize, albeit sometimes too late, that they've been extremely focused on providing and not much else. So I help guys to see that there's a whole relational, emotional role that they have in the marriage that they've probably not been tending to in the way that they could. In other words, they're not leading.

So once that clicks for a man, then the whole lens changes for them and they recognize, oh yeah, I really am not planning much. I'm really not being proactive in this way. I'm really not asking a lot of questions to sort of find out where she's at, what she's going through, what she's feeling. So it takes sometimes that crisis moment for a man to just be able to hear what he needs to hear. But the cool thing is once a guy sees it, then he'll want to lead because all men for the most part want to be a better leader.

**Aimee:** I feel that leadership is such an interesting angle and lens to perceive the behavior through because a lot of times as the women in the house, it seems like the more kids you have it's almost easier for the husband to fall in line with the children.

**Bart:** Yes.

**Aimee:** And then it's like mom becomes the leader. All the ducklings are following her. And so then dad just starts following her too. And it's like he would probably be happier in more of a leadership position in the domestic sphere, too. She would be happier with him being in the leadership position as well.

**Bart:** Now I want to defend men a little bit here because I know the journey that men have gone through when it comes to all of the hardships with marriage. I was married 15 years myself and have gone through the whole gamut and I'll just tell you that men's preparation for marriage is pretty much this: Bring home a paycheck and make sure nobody dies. That's kind of the plan.

That's kind of the plan that we go into marriage with. I'm being a bit facetious because of course we have connection with the woman in our life and there's emotional connection and we go on dates and vacations and things like that. But if you were to really ask a man what's the plan, he wouldn't have one. He really wouldn't have one. He's really going to be like, "Well, I'm going to provide for my wife and kids and protect them." And that's about as far as he's going to have planned.

And so I think it's important for women to just understand that that is his primary concern because that is what he's been taught by his family, society - that that's where his value lies. A man's value, right or wrong, is how much he can produce.

**Aimee:** And some men subscribe to that more than others.

**Bart:** But that's where the disconnect happens is he's thinking that that's the only thing that he needs to do. And also that that's going to make him happy.

**Aimee:** Doesn't always necessarily make him happy either because then you're sometimes super focused on making money, but maybe you don't have purpose or maybe you don't have other aspects of your life where you're growing your garden. You don't have time for friendships or have time for hobbies and those kinds of things too that can kind of burn a man out when you don't have good balance.

**Bart:** Yeah. One of the first things that we do when we bring a man into the program is just go, "Hey, there's eight mountains in your life." Call them mountains because you always feel like you're kind of climbing these things. So things like mind, body, career, companionship, hobbies and adventure, community or brotherhood, fatherhood or parenting and soul. I think that's eight.

And we just say you're not going to - the cruel trick of life is you're not going to be able to balance all of those. But you do need to have, if you don't have kids, okay, if you're not married, okay, so companionship and parenting, maybe those aren't active mountains that every man is climbing, but at some point typically they come into the picture and you have to figure out how to prioritize balancing those different eight mountains because they're all sort of required to have fulfillment or feel fulfillment.

So a lot of times guys get married and then they get lost in the marriage. I'm sure that's true for women as well.

**Aimee:** You do. You get totally lost in it. Sometimes you forget who you are completely. I wrote about it in my book after my first marriage. I was like, who am I? How do I even like my eggs cooked?

**Bart:** Yeah, right. So it happens for both men and women. And it's really helpful for another man to come alongside a guy who's maybe going through this kind of loss of self and just say hey remember she was attracted to the man that you once were before you were married, right? And so if you stopped being adventurous, you've kind of ceased with a lot of the hobbies that brought you joy, you sort of lost maybe your ambition to some degree. Now you're just trying to provide and play it safe. You've become somebody different than the man that she fell in love with.

And that can kind of be a light bulb moment. And so then a guy will go, "Oh, wow." I have to sort of hit the reset button a little bit here and rediscover that man that I once was so that I can bring that energy and fulfillment back into the marriage and the family.

**Aimee:** Yeah, absolutely. And the women do this - I did the same thing. I remember at one point in my second marriage, I was like, "Oh, whoa, we're going down the wrong road here again." And I'm like, "Oh, no." What can I do? Obviously, there's something about me. There's something I'm doing because this is now the second time this is happening to me. How - what do I need to own in this relationship? And realizing that I had really lost a lot of myself along the way. I really had to step back into who I was before we got married and get back to that place.

I definitely have a little bit of a strong woman persona but I can definitely get lost in fawning and servitude and being - let me just carry the cross around the house, I'm just a martyr. And I had to kind of take that off and go okay well this is who I need to be, this is what we need to do, we need to get this back together. We need to put this thing back on the tracks. And I had to realize that there was a lot of self ownership in my marriage. There was a lot of ownership on my side of the tracks that needed to be cleaned up too.

It was like you need - if you need him to do something, you need to communicate that very clearly with him so that he can do that because he's not a mind reader either. And a lot of times you get lost in the weeds of life and you can't even tell when you're getting into a behavior pattern that's not good, that's not healthy for you. And I feel like your partner kind of needs to come alongside and say, "Hey, that's not healthy for you. Let's try something else. Let's not do that. Let's try this a different way."

I do think that there's something in marriage that we have the opportunity to really help each other out and to be helpmates and to really be in a partnership. But it takes radical ownership and getting away from that place of fear and getting into that place of just radical love, not just for yourself but for your partner. Like, I love you too much to let you act this way. I've said that to my kids, but I definitely feel that in the relationship you could get to that place, too.

**Bart:** 100%. I help a lot of quote unquote nice guys. So the nice guy syndrome is something that a lot of people are aware of now, but maybe some aren't. And so I'll just sort of define it. A nice guy, sort of a misnomer because turns out not to be so nice a lot of the times, but he's a guy that is also, you could think of him as a pleaser who's trying not to rock the boat so that he can get the love and appreciation that he deeply desires.

So a nice guy was taught to try and get the gold star when he was younger. We're taught by primarily women teachers these days and have been - that's been the way for decades now because we moved from an agricultural society where boys were being raised by their fathers, uncles, grandfathers out in the fields, working - a little bit more exposure to men during the day. And then with the industrial revolution, things shifted and we started going to school. Men went to factories and then offices and so we're being raised by a lot of women teachers at school and then a lot of moms at home doing a lot of the raising.

And so the way that you keep kids and boys in check is you give them the gold star for doing a good job and following the rules. So you can imagine what that does when they get into a relationship and you're logging hours and hours and hours with this person. A man will a lot of times just naturally try and follow the rules, not make a mistake. And then what that ends up doing is they compromise themselves. They're scared to say no. They're scared to ask for what they want because they've learned to just try and follow the rules. So now they're following, not leading.

And then the repercussions of that are that they snap and they get angry because they feel like they've done so much for the other person and it's not being recognized and they feel justified in lashing out and then they look like Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde. Wait a minute.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Bart:** I thought you were this nice guy. No, you're actually acting super out of control and angry right now.

**Aimee:** Right. I know a lot of women that take on that same role in their relationships. And so it's funny. It's very - that's really one of the reasons I wanted to have you on the show because I think that there's this crossover of information because it's almost like we're dealing with - women were raised the same way by the same teachers being the same rule followers. So are we now getting into relationships without leadership?

**Bart:** That's right.

**Aimee:** Are we on a ship with no captain?

**Bart:** That's right. So we have to take extreme ownership and kind of reassess, wait a minute, what is my role here? Do I need to be saying no to certain things? So that's one of the things that we help guys do is develop a bit of a system or algorithm for that.

If I ask a guy what he stands for, I mean, he just generally won't have an answer. He goes off of his gut. And I believe those things that he stands for are actually in there. I believe that they're deep in there, but nobody sets aside the time to go, what are my principles? What are my non-negotiables?

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Bart:** But once you have those clear, it really helps with making decisions because you can go, "Oh, wait a minute. That violates a non-negotiable in my life. I need to say no to that or I need to stand for that."

A lot of what I do is I say you stand for something like boundaries. I think boundaries is a really popular concept in our culture that was developed I want to say in the 70s by therapists and boundaries obviously did wonders for a lot of people and they're important. But when I'm coaching a man on how to be a leader, boundaries are not my favorite way of explaining boundaries. I sort of reframe. And here's why.

Because boundaries, the context in which they were originated are out of kind of the therapist's office. And a therapist is going to ask a lot of questions, get you to open up, share your feelings, things like that. Nothing wrong with that, but that for a man isn't always the most helpful thing. We were raised to kind of get coaching. The coach tells you how to hit the ball. The coach tells you how to throw the ball. The coach shows you how to tighten up your stroke, whatever it is, right? And we actually like that as men because we just want to do it right. We want to go out there and see the results.

**Aimee:** Yeah, that makes sense. I have two boys that are kinesthetic learners and they - I don't think they would learn from therapy, talk therapy the way that they would learn from some sort of more physical coaching.

**Bart:** Right. Right.

**Aimee:** That makes sense to me.

**Bart:** So there's nothing wrong with therapy, but if you're looking to dramatically improve in a short amount of time, a lot of times you benefit from just having a guy say, "You're not doing it right. Here's a better way. Try this." Then they do it and they go, "Why have I been doing it the wrong way for so long?" Well, you were just never taught. So now you know, and does that feel better? Yeah. Oh, it turns out I didn't have to sit and talk about my feelings for three sessions. I just needed to be told what to do.

**Aimee:** Exactly. And that's just - it's the way we're wired. You're so right. Guys and men and women can tend to be wired a little differently. I can see how sometimes it's so much easier - can we cut to the chase and just show me how to do things the right way rather than wandering through the stories of life in the past.

**Bart:** Right. So what I was going to say about boundaries is that sometimes boundaries get confusing for men because of course we need boundaries. We have laws. We have rules. So there are things that there's consequences for doing things that are not right and there should be. But when you're leading in a relationship as a man, if you have a bunch of boundaries, it can get - it can just instill more kind of fear at what I'll call kind of a fear-driven atmosphere. Hey, if you do this, I'm going to walk out of the room or whatever.

So there's a time and place for that. If you start yelling or whatever, I'm going to walk out of the room. Okay, there's a time and place for that. But before that, I want guys to think about boundaries as principles. So principles are a little bit more positive. I say they should be sort of us-oriented.

So if you're going to raise your voice and start yelling, that's not good. It's not good for the kids, right? But if you have a principle that is, I bring emotional, physical, financial protection for my family, that's a great principle to have. Now, you come in when the yelling starts or whatever, you let things cool down, but you say, "Hey, look, this is not something that I want for us, for our house, for our children. It's damaging. And so I want to take us to a new chapter or place where when we get really heated, we decide to take a time out, whatever. And it's always going to be no more than 20 minutes before we come back."

The details are not important. But what I'm a really big fan of is a guy explaining why it's of benefit to her and the relationship and the family and sort of standing for that thing instead of framing it as I don't like it when you do that. It hurts me or it makes me upset or whatever and I'm just going to walk away. No. Talk about why if we do this, it has harmful repercussions and I want to elevate us to the next level. That's leadership.

**Aimee:** I love that. And that's coming from a place of safety for her and safety for her children, which speaks to women on a very deep primal level.

**Bart:** Yeah.

**Aimee:** We deeply want to feel that our partner desperately and above all wants to keep us safe.

**Bart:** That's right.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Bart:** Yeah. And hold me to the same standard, too. Right. So that's kind of the thing I think about principles that are a little bit more designed for men in the sense that we as leaders, if we're trying to lead, a principle is something that's applicable to ourselves and others, right? So we need to be held accountable as well for the same thing. Whereas boundaries - I'm not saying they're bad. I'm just saying as a man, a boundary can feel a little bit like I'm trying to protect myself.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Bart:** Right. And that doesn't feel as - I can see how a man could feel threatened. Yeah. Yeah. I can see how that word is threatening. It doesn't sound very loving.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Bart:** Yeah. Yeah. I can totally - yeah. So yeah. Absolutely.

**Aimee:** You're talking - you're working with men who are coming kind of from that place of like fear and avoidance in a relationship. You're working with guys who are kind of in that space at one time and now from a female perspective, that kind of looks like stonewalling and silence. A lot of times when the guy is like on that side of like, oh, I'm afraid, I'm going to avoid, this is going to start a fight or this is uncomfortable. So could you break down what's actually going on inside a man in those moments and how a woman could respond without taking on more emotional work?

**Bart:** Sure. I just want to be clear about the boundaries thing because I want to make sure that the audience understands I'm fine with women having boundaries. I think if a woman has boundaries in relationships, that's really important and those should be honored. All I'm saying is that for men to sort of mirror that boundary game, I think creates a funky dynamic.

**Aimee:** Totally. Yeah. I love Terri Cole's book, Boundary Boss. And for women who come from the codependent world, which I definitely came - I think codependence was modeled to me. It was probably modeled to my mother. And definitely becoming aware of the fact that I'm a high functioning codependent and reading Terri's book and kind of going through - she'll have you do your list of things that are absolutely non-negotiable for me and all of these things and it really comes from a place of principles but we definitely have been using the word boundaries in that terminology for sure.

**Bart:** Yeah.

**Aimee:** Okay cool. So back to your question.

**Bart:** What's going on for men is at the root of it is typically shame.

**Aimee:** Okay.

**Bart:** So shame looks like I'm not doing a good job. I'm not good enough. I'm not a good husband. I'm not getting the gold star.

**Aimee:** Oh, it's so true. Shame is such a dark but very present emotion in our society. I feel like - I don't know if it's ever been more prevalent. I think that's why we're dealing with so many mental health issues like the depression and the addiction that our society's dealing with right now. It seems like even the eating addictions and all of those things really come from a place of shame.

**Bart:** Yep. So the dead giveaway that shame is at the root of things is shame wants to isolate. So you talk about stonewalling or avoiding, pulling away. I see this a lot, right? Somebody keeps pulling away. Somebody keeps avoiding or they're stonewalling.

Stonewalling can be a necessary tactic if you're dealing with somebody who's narcissistic, whatever. There's a time and place for stonewalling. Unfortunately, there's a lot of people who are misdiagnosing others as narcissistic in our society because it's such a buzzword on social media. And I think it's sad. So I really hold guys accountable to that. I say don't label, don't diagnose people as being this or that. All that's doing is kind of offloading your responsibility to change and uplevel. And even if they are a narcissist, how are you going to hold yourself responsible to do the healthy thing in the midst of their unhealthy behavior?

**Aimee:** That's right.

**Bart:** Shame causes us to want to isolate and we're going to withdraw. We're going to avoid. We're going to stonewall. We're going to be silent. And I know for me, I remember the feeling of I just don't want to do anything wrong again.

**Aimee:** And so I'm just not going to do anything.

**Bart:** It's so sad to me that there's like this massive communication breakdown in so many marriages because marriage should be this really powerful educational tool where you really get to learn who you are, expand your understanding of yourself and your partner. I think marriage has tremendous potential but without - with shame and then just a lack of communication or understanding empathy for what our partners are feeling there's - it can really digress into something that's just a yucky ugly power play.

**Bart:** Yeah. And I would say the antidote is of course forgiveness and reconciliation. So you have to believe that you're forgiven. I see a lot of people who don't want to forgive themselves and they don't want to forgive others and they certainly don't know how to reconcile. And so that's another thing that we teach men how to do is to do reconciliation properly.

And one of the most common mistakes I see with men in terms of - when they make a mistake, they'll kind of go back to her and they'll apologize, but then they'll justify and they'll start going into why they did it. And that's just not actually an apology at the end of the day, right? It's just an explanation. But I kind of - you own it and just say I'm sorry. I'm owning this behavior. I did it. I'm sorry. And I own exactly what I did.

**Aimee:** Yeah. It must have felt terrible.

**Bart:** I see this now.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Bart:** I want to make the - I'm going to make the changes.

**Aimee:** Yeah.

**Bart:** And you watch, and I want to be held accountable to it and here's the plan.

**Aimee:** Right. Yeah.
